subject: Social Science
title: Print Culture and the Modern World

questions.text: The earliest kind of print technology was developed in which countries?
questions.explanation: This was a system of hand printing using woodblocks.

questions.text: The method of 'woodblock printing' involved:
options: Rubbing paper against the inked surface of woodblocks
questions.explanation: Paper was rubbed against woodblocks which had raised, inked surfaces.

questions.text: The oldest Japanese book, printed in 868 AD, is the:
questions.explanation: The Diamond Sutra contains six sheets of text and woodcut illustrations.

questions.text: Who brought the knowledge of woodblock printing to Europe from China?
questions.explanation: Marco Polo returned to Italy in 1295 after many years of exploration in China.

questions.text: Vellum is a writing surface made from:
questions.explanation: Vellum was expensive and used for luxury editions before paper became common.

questions.text: Who developed the first known printing press in the 1430s?
questions.explanation: Gutenberg was a German goldsmith who adapted the olive press design for printing.

questions.text: The first book printed by Gutenberg on his press was the:
questions.explanation: About 180 copies were printed in three years, which was very fast for that time.

questions.text: The 'Print Revolution' refers to:
options: A new way of producing books that transformed the lives of people
questions.explanation: It changed the relationship of information with knowledge and with institutions and authorities.

questions.text: Who wrote the 'Ninety Five Theses' in 1517, challenging the Catholic Church?
questions.explanation: Luther's writings led to the Protestant Reformation.

questions.text: What was the 'Index of Prohibited Books'?
options: A list maintained by the Catholic Church to control heretical ideas
questions.explanation: From 1558, the Church began to maintain this index to prevent the spread of ideas it disagreed with.

questions.text: In the 17th and 18th centuries, literacy rates in Europe:
questions.explanation: Churches set up schools, and print made reading accessible to the common people.

questions.text: Penny Chapbooks were:
options: Small books sold by travelling pedlars for a penny
questions.explanation: They were popular in England and allowed even the poor to buy and read books.

questions.text: In France, the 'Bibliotheque Bleue' were:
options: Low-priced small books printed on poor quality paper
questions.explanation: These were named after their cheap blue covers.

questions.text: Which movement was aided by the spread of print, challenging the 'despotic' power of the French monarchy?
questions.explanation: Print popularised the ideas of Enlightenment thinkers like Voltaire and Rousseau.

questions.text: Who said 'Printing is the ultimate gift of God and the greatest one'?
questions.explanation: Luther recognized print as the primary tool for spreading religious and reformist ideas.

questions.text: The first printing press in India was brought to Goa by:
questions.explanation: Jesuit priests learned Konkani and printed several tracts in the mid-16th century.

questions.text: The 'Bengal Gazette', a weekly magazine that described itself as 'a commercial paper open to all, but influenced by none', was started by:
questions.explanation: Hickey's paper was independent and often critical of the Company government.

questions.text: Raja Rammohun Roy published the ______ in 1821 to spread his ideas of reform.
questions.explanation: It was a Persian/Bengali journal used to debate social and religious issues.

questions.text: The Deoband Seminary, founded in 1867, published thousands of ______ telling Muslim readers how to conduct themselves.
questions.explanation: These provided guidance on everyday life and religious interpretations in the face of colonial influence.

questions.text: Which book, written by Jyotiba Phule in 1871, focused on the injustices of the caste system?
questions.explanation: Phule dedicated this book to the American movement to free slaves.

questions.text: The first full-length autobiography in Bengali, 'Amar Jiban', was written by:
questions.explanation: A housewife from a rich zamindar family, she learned to read in secret and wrote about her life.

questions.text: The Vernacular Press Act of 1878 was modeled on the:
questions.explanation: It gave the government extensive powers to censor reports and editorials in the vernacular press.

questions.text: In the 19th century, Richard M. Hoe of New York perfected the:
questions.explanation: This press could print 8,000 sheets per hour, making newspapers much cheaper.

questions.text: Why were manuscripts not widely used in everyday life in India before the mid-19th century?
options: They were written in a difficult script
questions.explanation: Manuscripts were handwritten on palm leaves or handmade paper and were not for mass use.

questions.text: A 'Heretic' is someone who:
options: Holds beliefs that do not conform to the established teachings of the Church
questions.explanation: Heresy was seen by the Church as a threat to its authority.

questions.text: The term 'Calligraphy' refers to:
options: The art of beautiful and stylised writing
questions.explanation: Before printing, all books were created through skilled calligraphy.

questions.text: The 'Shamlal Akhbar' and 'Jam-i-Jahan Nama' were early newspapers in:
questions.explanation: Persian was an important language of administration and culture in early 19th century India.

questions.text: Which leader was arrested in 1908 for writing about Punjabi revolutionaries in his paper 'Kesari'?
questions.explanation: Tilak's arrest led to widespread protests across India.

questions.text: Print culture allowed women to:
options: Become authors and express their feelings
questions.explanation: Print provided a platform for women to enter the public sphere and debate their rights.

questions.text: The transformation of the reading public into a hearing public was due to:
options: Reading aloud of books at gatherings for those who couldn't read
questions.explanation: Printers began publishing popular ballads and folk tales with illustrations to reach non-literates.

questions.text: Erasmus, a Latin scholar, was highly ________ of the print revolution.
questions.explanation: Erasmus feared that the abundance of books would lead to the spread of 'scandalous and irreligious' ideas.

questions.text: The 'Galley' in a printing press is:
options: A metal frame in which types are set and the text composed
questions.explanation: It is a technical term from the era of manual typesetting.

questions.text: In 19th century Europe, novels became a popular medium for:
options: Exploring the lives of ordinary people and social problems
questions.explanation: Novels like those by Jane Austen or Charles Dickens reflected the changing society.

questions.text: The first printed books in Europe closely resembled ______ in appearance.
questions.explanation: Printers used metal types that imitated the hand-written styles of the time.

questions.text: What was the result of the 'Print Revolution' on oral culture?
options: Oral and print cultures blurred as printed material was read aloud
questions.explanation: The boundaries between the two were not sharp; hearing and reading overlapped.

questions.text: Catholic priests first printed books in the ______ language in 1579 at Cochin.
questions.explanation: Tamil was one of the first Indian languages to have printed materials.

questions.text: The 'Akashvani' was later known as:
questions.explanation: While not part of early print, it is part of the broader communication history.

questions.text: The invention of printing led to the birth of a 'public sphere' where:
options: Ordinary people could debate and discuss ideas
questions.explanation: Reason and logic replaced blind faith as people discussed new ideas.

questions.text: The term 'Despotism' refers to a system of government in which:
options: The ruler has absolute and unchecked power
questions.explanation: Print culture helped in delegitimising the basis of such absolute power.

questions.text: Lithography is a method of printing from a:
options: Stone or metal plate with a smooth surface
questions.explanation: It became popular in India for producing cheap religious and decorative prints.
